intent: NEGATIVE FIXTURE: a role dyntransition denial (newrole_t self, role staff_r -> system_r). The canonical role-constraint case from the permissive-probe. The TE allow exists, so the role constraint is the sole binding reason. RuleSteward must DETECT-and-DECLINE.
_kinds_note: BOTH layers agree: authoritative=Constraint (role constrain DENIED), floor=RoleSuspected (scontext role staff_r vs tcontext role system_r mismatch). RBAC collapses into Constraint (f4 section 8).

oracle.audit2allow_N: allow newrole_t self:process dyntransition;  (BOGUS - role constraint blocks regardless of the TE allow)
oracle.audit2allow_R: same BOGUS allow newrole_t self:process dyntransition; plus the constraint comment block
oracle.narrower_than_R_by: N/A - decline scenario. expected.te is rule-free and compiles exit 0.
oracle.audit2why_detail: constrain process { transition noatsecure siginh rlimitinh dyntransition } ((r1 == r2 -Fail-) or ... (t1 == can_system_change -Fail-) and (r2 == system_r) or (t1 == process_uncond_exempt -Fail-)); Constraint DENIED. Possible cause is the source role (staff_r) and target role (system_r) are different.

triage_expectation.should_decline_with_reason: RBAC/role constraint violation: source role staff_r differs from target role system_r on a process dyntransition. A TE allow cannot fix a role constraint; the fix is an RBAC change.
triage_expectation.suspected_trap: A naive impl emits the BOGUS allow newrole_t self:process dyntransition; (the TE allow already exists, so this changes nothing). The floor's RoleSuspected flag catches it offline.
